content.full_text:
July 21, 1989

Helen A. Ramos, CMC
Office of the City Clerk
650 N. La Cadena Drive
Colton, CA 92324

RE: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-89-340

Dear Ms. Ramos:

You have requested advice regarding the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because your request
is a general inquiry, we treat your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/

QUESTION

May incumbent officeholders expend campaign funds to
purchase tickets to dinners honoring other elected officials and
to sponsor a "golf hole" for a golf tournament sponsored by a
local non-profit community organization?

CONCLUSION

The Act prohibits transfers of campaign funds between
candidates and officeholders and limits the use of campaign funds
received prior to January 1, 1989. The Act does not otherwise
specify permissible uses of campaign funds. Therefore, if a
dinner "honoring" another elected official is a campaign
fundraiser for the official, or would result in a transfer of
funds to the official, incumbent officeholders may not expend

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Helen A. Ramos, CMC
Page 2


campaign funds for that event. Likewise, if the purpose of the
golf tournament is to raise funds to make contributions to
candidates, incumbent officeholders may not use campaign funds to
sponsor a golf hole.

If the two events would not result in a contribution
prohibited by the Act, the legality of the payments is governed by
Elections Code Sections 12400-12405. Questions regarding the
Elections Code may be addressed to your district attorney or the
state Attorney General.

ANALYSIS

Officeholders may not use campaign funds to make
contributions to other candidates or officeholders. The Political
Reform Act, as amended by Proposition 73, prohibits transfers of
campaign funds between candidates. Section 85304 states in part:

     No candidate for elective office or committee
     controlled by that candidate or candidates
     for elective office shall transfer any contri-
     bution to any other candidate for elective
     office.....

In addition, campaign funds received prior to January 1,
1989, which do not fall within the contribution limitations
imposed by Proposition 73 ("restricted funds") may not be used to
make contributions to, or independent expenditures to support or
oppose, candidates or officeholders. (Section 85306; Regulation
18536.2.)3/


3/ Section 85306 prohibits the use of campaign funds received
prior to January 1, 1989, to support or oppose a candidacy. On
May 15, 1989, a federal court issued a preliminary injunction
prohibiting the Commission from enforcing Section 85306 as to
contributions raised prior to January 1, 1989, in amounts that
fall within Proposition 73's contribution limitations. (Service
Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, CLC et al. v. FPPC, Case
No. CIVS 89-0422-LKK-JFM, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of
California.) A final hearing on this issue is scheduled for
August 11, 1989. Funds received by a candidate or officeholder
prior to January 1, 1989, which are not covered by Section 85306
because they fall within the contribution limitations are still
covered by the prohibition on transfers between candidates
contained in Section 85304.

Helen A. Ramos, CMC
Page 3

Therefore, any payment made from an officeholder's campaign
funds that would result in a contribution or other transfer to
another state or local candidate or officeholder is prohibited.

With regard to other types of payments made from campaign
funds, such as charitable donations, Elections Code Section 12400
prohibits the "personal use" of campaign funds and states that
payments made from campaign funds must have "more than a
negligible political, legislative, or governmental purpose."
Enclosed is a copy of an advisory letter from the Attorney
General's office concerning use of campaign funds for charitable
contributions. To determine if the payments you have questioned
would be allowed under the Elections Code, you should contact the
state Attorney General or your district attorney.

Please contact me at 916/322-5662 if you have further
questions regarding this advice.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

colton                                                    OFFICE OF THE
                                                   Department: CITY CLERK


May 25, 1989                                       Phone No.: (714) 370-5034




Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street - Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attention: John McLean

Dear Mr. McLean:

Your assistance is requested on the following commonly asked
questions:

1. Can incumbent officeholders expend campaign funds during
   election year and/or off-year to participate in the follow-
   ing:

   a. To purchase tickets to dinner honoring another
      elected official.

   b. To sponsor a "Golf Hole" for golf tournament
      sponsored by local, non-profit, community organi-
      zation.

   Are these types of expenditures considered qualified campaign
   expenditures and/or campaign strategy expenses for election to
   a future term of office?

Your assistance on the above is greatly appreciated.

Very truly yours,

Helen A. Ramos
HELEN A. RAMOS, CMC
City Clerk



CITY OF COLTON CIVIC CENTER  •  650 N. LA CADENA DRIVE  •  COLTON, CALIFORNIA 92324

colton                                                    OFFICE OF THE
                                                     Department: CITY CLERK


                    May 25, 1989                      Phone No.:  (714) 370-5034


                    Fair Political Practices Commission
                    Legal Division
                    428 J Street - Suite 800
                    Sacramento, CA  95814

                    Attention:  John McLean

                    Dear Mr. McLean:

                    Your assistance is requested on the following commonly asked
                    questions:

                    1.  Can incumbent officeholders expend campaign funds during
                        election year and/or off-year to participate in the follow-
                        ing:

                        a.  To purchase tickets to dinner honoring another
                            elected official.

                        b.  To sponsor a "Golf Hole" for golf tournament
                            sponsored by local, non-profit, community organi-
                            zation.

                    Are these types of expenditures considered qualified campaign
                    expenditures and/or campaign strategy expenses for election to
                    a future term of office?

                    Your assistance on the above is greatly appreciated.

                    Very truly yours,

                    Helen A. Ramos
                    HELEN A. RAMOS, CMC
                    City Clerk


CITY OF COLTON CIVIC CENTER  •  650 N. LA CADENA DRIVE  •  COLTON, CALIFORNIA 92324

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 1, 1989


Helen Ramos
Office of the City Clerk
City of Colton Civic Center
650 N. La Cadena Drive
Colton

Re:  Letter No. 89-340

Dear Ms. Ramos:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on May 30, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission.  If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice.  If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to the
information needed.  If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can.  (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Joanne Pritchard
Joanne Pritchard
Chief Technical Assistance
and Analysis Division
